sentences.base_language: It's early morning and the bakery smells wonderful.
sentences.target_language: Es temprano por la mañana y la panadería huele de maravilla.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Es temprano** uses **ser** for time expressions — time of day is always expressed with ser, not estar.
**Huele** = it smells (third-person singular of **oler**, o→ue stem-changing verb). An **h** is added in stem-changed forms because Spanish words can't begin with the diphthong **ue**: huelo, hueles, huele.
**La panadería** — the suffix **-ería** on a food word typically means 'a shop selling that thing': panadería (bread shop), carnicería (butcher), frutería (fruit shop), pastelería (pastry shop).

sentences.base_language: Carlos walks in and sees all kinds of bread and pastries.
sentences.target_language: Carlos entra y ve todo tipo de panes y pan dulce.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Pan dulce** = sweet pastries / baked goods — broad term used especially in Mexico and Central America.
In Spain: **bollería** or **pastelería**. **Panadería** = the bakery; **pastelería** = pastry shop.

sentences.base_language: 'Good morning! What's fresh out of the oven today?'
sentences.target_language: —¡Buenos días! ¿Qué tiene recién salido del horno hoy?
sentences.grammarNote:
**Buenos días** = good morning — literally 'good days' (plural). Compare: **buenas tardes** (good afternoon), **buenas noches** (good evening/night).
**Del horno** is the mandatory contraction of **de + el**: **de + el horno = del horno**. Whenever **de** precedes the article **el**, they must contract to **del**.
**¿Qué tiene...?** uses **usted** form (**tiene** = you have) — formal register appropriate for a customer addressing staff.

sentences.base_language: 'The croissants just came out five minutes ago,' says the baker.
sentences.target_language: —Los cuernitos acaban de salir hace cinco minutos —dice la panadera.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Acabar de + infinitive** = to have just done something. Acaban de salir = they just came out.
acabo de comer (I just ate), acaba de llegar (she just arrived)

sentences.base_language: 'Oh, they look delicious. I'll take two croissants, please.'
sentences.target_language: —Ay, qué ricos se ven. Me llevo dos cuernitos, por favor.
sentences.grammarNote:
**¡Qué ricos se ven!** = 'How delicious they look!' — **qué + adjective** expresses an exclamation.
**Se ven** is a reflexive/middle construction: literally 'they see themselves (as)' — used to describe appearance.
**Me llevo** = I'll take (lit. 'I take for myself') — using **llevar** reflexively implies taking something away. Very common in shops instead of **compro** (I buy).

sentences.base_language: 'Would you also like to try the pan de elote? It came out this morning.'
sentences.target_language: —¿También quiere probar el pan de elote? Salió esta mañana.
sentences.grammarNote: **¿Quiere probar...?** uses **querer** (stem-changing: quiero, quieres, quiere) + probar (to try/taste). Very common for offering samples in Mexican bakeries and markets.

sentences.base_language: 'Sure, I'll try a piece. It smells amazing.'
sentences.target_language: —Claro, voy a probar un pedazo. Huele increíble.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Voy a probar** = near future (ir a + infinitive): 'I'm going to try.' This is the most common way to express near-future actions in everyday Spanish, more so than the simple future (**probaré**).
**Huele increíble** — note no **a** needed here: **oler** can directly take an adjective describing smell quality.

sentences.base_language: Carlos tastes it and his eyes light up.
sentences.target_language: Carlos lo prueba y se le iluminan los ojos.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Lo prueba** — **lo** is the direct object pronoun (it, masc.), placed before the conjugated verb.
**Se le iluminan los ojos** = his eyes light up — a very common Spanish construction for involuntary physical reactions: **se + indirect object pronoun (le) + verb + body part**. Compare: **se me cayó** (I dropped it / it fell from me), **se le olvidó** (he forgot it, lit. 'it forgot itself to him').

sentences.base_language: 'That's so good! I'll take one of those too. Can I get it to go?'
sentences.target_language: —¡Qué rico está! Me llevo uno también. ¿Me lo puede poner para llevar?
sentences.grammarNote:
**Para llevar** = to go (takeaway).
**Para comer aquí** = to eat here. Both phrases work in bakeries, cafés, and restaurants throughout the Spanish-speaking world.

sentences.base_language: 'Of course! That'll be forty-five pesos in total.'
sentences.target_language: —¡Claro! Son cuarenta y cinco pesos en total.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Son** (from **ser**) is used for prices and totals: **¿Cuánto es?** / **Son diez pesos**. Spanish uses **ser** for prices as an expression of equivalence ('they are forty-five pesos'), not estar.
**Cuarenta y cinco** — Spanish connects tens and units with **y** (and): 21 = veintiuno, 31 = treinta y uno, 45 = cuarenta y cinco.

sentences.base_language: Carlos pays and leaves happy with a bag of warm bread.
sentences.target_language: Carlos paga y sale feliz con una bolsa de pan caliente.
sentences.grammarNote:
**Sale** = he leaves (third-person singular of **salir**, to leave/go out). **Salir** has an irregular yo form: **salgo**.
**Feliz** (happy) is placed after the verb here as a predicate adjective describing Carlos's state as he leaves — a very natural construction in Spanish narrative. Note **feliz** stays the same for masculine/feminine singular; plural: **felices**.
